items.headline: Software disruption is already broad and measurable
items.summary: Track utility and labor effects in writing, coding, support, research, and operations rather than waiting for a single AGI arrival date.

public.summary: The fastest-moving disruption is software work that can be decomposed into repetitive reasoning and execution loops.
public.implication: The important question now is measured utility and labor impact, not binary AGI declarations.
public.analogy: Less like a moon landing and more like electrification spreading unevenly across firms.

analyst.context: Utilization and workflow substitution are more informative than model demo quality.

engineer.technicalDetail: Reliability gains are real in bounded tasks, but end-to-end autonomy still needs human fallback paths.
engineer.bottleneck: Ground-truth evaluation for open-ended workflows lags deployment speed.

items.headline: Labor-market stress broadens in the late 2020s
items.summary: Expect painful reallocation and bargaining pressure before any clean macro collapse narrative.

public.summary: Sector-specific automation pressure becomes harder to dismiss as isolated churn.
public.implication: Transition dynamics matter: retraining, wage compression, and role redesign can be severe without total collapse.

analyst.context: Watch cross-industry consistency rather than single-company layoffs.

engineer.technicalDetail: Automation value scales when orchestration, QA, and exception handling are integrated into production systems.
engineer.bottleneck: Institutional adaptation is slower than model iteration cycles.

items.headline: Physical-economy disruption becomes plausible in early 2030s
items.summary: Robotics momentum is meaningful, but should not be treated as proof of immediate economy-wide humanoid saturation.

public.summary: The first broad physical disruption window likely requires years of deployment learning.
public.implication: Autonomous logistics and industrial robotics can scale before general-purpose humanoids are ubiquitous.

analyst.context: Pilot success is not equivalent to mass deployment economics.

engineer.technicalDetail: Durability, maintenance intervals, and safety certification dominate scaling pace.
engineer.bottleneck: Field reliability under noisy, long-horizon operations remains unresolved at scale.

items.headline: After 2035, use scenarios not precise forecasts
items.summary: Energy build-out, supply chains, regulation, and reliability become dominant uncertainty drivers.

public.summary: Beyond 2035 the uncertainty fan widens, so deterministic date claims become less credible.
public.implication: Planning should shift to contingencies and stress tests across energy, policy, and logistics constraints.

analyst.context: Confidence should decrease as horizon length and system complexity increase.

engineer.technicalDetail: Long-range outcomes depend on non-ML bottlenecks as much as model capability curves.
engineer.bottleneck: Power availability and regulated deployment surfaces can dominate pure algorithmic progress.
